CONCEPT structure of product accountability report

The following chapter layout shows you a possible layout of your product accountability report. This structure is based on the SDLC. The structure is deliberately not complete, and it is certainly not the only structure that is suitable. Also read the graduation manual for tips and use the book 'Writing for technicians'.

Foreword Summary Glossary

Introduction

problem analysis
Company description
Business orientation (only for internship)
Context and system landscape
Issue
Goal
Possible solutions
Stakeholders

Approach
Methodologies
Conditions
Risk analysis
Planning

Requirements Analyse
Functional requirements
Non-functional requirements
Preconditions/frameworks
Technology choices

Architecture and design
Introduction/approach
5.2 …

Realization
Development Street
6.2 …

To test

A Product Accountability Report is a comprehensive document that details how a particular product was conceived, developed, tested, and launched, with an analysis of its performance and impact. The structure you've provided is indeed based on the Software Development Life Cycle (SDLC), but it can be adapted for reporting on a variety of products. Here’s an enhanced version of the structure with some additional details:

Foreword
- Acknowledgements, purpose, and scope of the report.

Summary
- An executive summary highlighting the key findings, outcomes, and recommendations.

Glossary
- Definitions of technical terms, acronyms, and abbreviations used in the report.

Introduction
- Background information, the rationale for the project, objectives, and the structure of the report.

Problem Analysis
1.1. Company Description
- An overview of the company, its mission, and core values.
1.2. Business Orientation (only for internship)
- The business perspective or involvement in the product's development (if applicable).
1.3. Context and System Landscape
- The environment in which the product operates, including existing systems and infrastructure.
1.4. Issue
- Identification of the primary issue or need that the product addresses.
1.5. Goal
- Clear articulation of the product's intended outcomes and objectives.
1.6. Possible Solutions
- Exploration of alternative solutions or strategies considered.
1.7. Stakeholders
- Identification and analysis of stakeholders affected by or interested in the product.

Approach
2.1. Methodologies
- Overview of the methodologies used in the product's development (e.g., Agile, Waterfall).
2.2. Conditions
- Constraints and prerequisites for the product's development.
2.3. Risk Analysis
- Identification and assessment of potential risks associated with the product.
2.4. Planning
- Project timeline, milestones, and scheduling details.

Requirements Analysis
3.1. Functional Requirements
- Detailed description of the product's functional specifications and capabilities.
3.2. Non-functional Requirements
- Specifications regarding usability, reliability, performance, and supportability.
3.3. Preconditions/Frameworks
- Preconditions and guiding principles that shaped the product development.
3.4. Technology Choices
- Justification of technology stack and tools selected.

Architecture and Design
4.1. Introduction/Approach
- The conceptual framework and strategies used in the product's design.
4.2. [Specific Design Elements]
- In-depth explanation of the product's architecture and design components.

Realization
5.1. Development Street
- Description of the development process, including iterations, coding standards, and version control.
5.2. [Implementation Details]
- Specifics on the implementation phases, integration, and customization efforts.

To Test
6.1. Test Strategy
- Outline of the testing methodologies, types of tests conducted, and test environments.
6.2. Report
- Summary of test results, issues encountered, and resolutions.
6.3. Metrics
- Quantitative measures of the testing process and product quality.

Results
- Presentation of the project outcomes, deliverables, and performance metrics.

Conclusion
7.1. Evaluation
- Critical assessment of the project's success in meeting its goals and solving the identified issue.
7.2. Recommendations
- Suggestions for future improvements, upgrades, or research.

POP/Reflection
- Personal reflection on the project process, learning outcomes, and personal development.

Appendices
- Additional supporting information such as detailed charts, code snippets, or extended data.

Sources
- Comprehensive list of references, including literature, articles, and other resources cited.

Attachments
- Any supplementary material not included in the main body of the report, such as user manuals, technical specifications, or raw data.

Remember, depending on the nature of the product and the audience for the report, some sections may be expanded, merged, or omitted. The structure should serve as a guide and be tailored to best communicate the information pertinent to the product and its stakeholders.

give me now the feedback if this respect the structure that i give to you "Introduction
This report presents an in-depth analysis and implementation of a project at companyX aimed at optimizing collaboration through the integration of OPAL, Microsoft Teams and Azure DevOps. The underlying goal is to promote efficient communication and structured project management within the organization.

Over OPAL, Teams & DevOps
OPAL, as the internal project management platform, is the core of the business. This report highlights the strategic use of OPAL in combination with Microsoft Teams and Azure DevOps, resulting in an integrated work environment in which communication, development and project management come together.
The communications hub, Microsoft Teams, is used to connect customers and team members directly and at the same time serves as a virtual workshop where innovative ideas are developed. On the technical side, Azure DevOps provides a structured environment for detailed project development, using Scrum boards, code repositories, and build/release pipelines.

Flexibility and Efficiency in Harmony
This project stands out because of the flexibility that the synchronization tool offers. The ability to seamlessly sync data between OPAL, Teams and DevOps enables companyX to quickly meet the specific requirements of each project. The report not only highlights the technical implementation of the synchronization tool, but also shows how it contributes to a streamlined and consistent workflow, where the balance between flexibility and efficiency is central.

This introduction provides a preview of the pages to come, in which we will explore in detail the initiation, implementation, and results of this specific project.
Terminology clarification
This report provides an in-depth analysis of the sync tool development process, often referring to terms such as “Teams Notifications,” the “Teams List,” “DevOps Notification Board,” and “DevOps Notifications.” To avoid possible confusion, it is important to clarify the specific, technical meaning of these terms:
Teams notifications: Indicates individual items in a SharePoint list that serve as notifications within Teams. These items are managed in a specific SharePoint list known as the "Teams list".
Teams list: Refers to the SharePoint list where the Teams notifications are recorded. It serves as the central hub for managing and updating all notifications.
DevOps-meldingenbord: Corresponding to Azure Boards, DevOps notification boards represent the place where DevOps Work Items are tracked and managed. These Work Items include several variations that are important for the development process.
The DevOps message: Indicates specific Work Items within the DevOps notification board, which range from tasks to user stories and bugs.

The introduction you've provided captures the essence of the aims and mechanisms involved in the integration of OPAL, Microsoft Teams, and Azure DevOps within companyX. Below is some feedback that respects the structure you initiall provided, places the introduction within that structure, and takes into account writing conventions suitable for a technical report.

- **Clarity and Structure:** The introduction clearly states the objective of the project—**to optimize collaboration within companyX**—and sets the stage for discussion of OPAL, Teams, and DevOps. It is well structured and adheres to the initial format of presenting an overview before diving into details.

- **Terminology Clarification:** The section on terminology is useful, particularly for readers who may not be familiar with the specific tools and terms used in the project. However, the term "DevOps-meldingenbord" might be a typo or language inconsistency, as it switches from English to another language (Dutch, which would translate to "DevOps notifications board"). Make sure all terminologies are presented in a consistent language unless specifically addressing an audience that would understand the mixed usage.

- **Content**: The content thus far aligns with the structure presented. **However**, to follow your initial structure strictly, consider adding more context about the company's background and the specific problem this integration seeks to address in the **problem analysis** section, if not already included elsewhere.

Here are some specific suggestions:

1. Consider expanding on the **problem analysis** to provide the reader more background on why the synchronization tool was necessary. What were the problems or inefficiencies that prompted this initiative?

2. Reflect on the structure provided in the initial outline and ensure that subsequent sections will cover:
   - The business orientation (why this matters to the company's mission and strategy).
   - The context in terms of system landscape prior to integration.
   - The goals and solutions, both potential and chosen.
   - Key stakeholders affected and their roles.

3. Use your **Approach** section later in the report to discuss the methodologies, conditions, and risk analysis pertinent to the synchronization tool's development.

Remember to maintain **consistency** in how you reference terms throughout the report, and to expand on abbreviations upon first usage so that readers can become quickly acquainted with key terms.

Overall, the current draft provides a coherent and enticing introduction for readers to understand what is to come in the report, mentions essential details regarding the integrated tools, and sets the stage for a detailed analysis of the project's development and impact.

problem analysis
This chapter describes the causes, consequences and context of the problem so that the problem can be better identified and analyzed. First of all, the reason for the problem is formulated in paragraph 3.1. In section 3.2 the problem that needs to be solved is formulated. In section 3.3, possible solutions to the problem are identified and discussed. Section 3.5 identifies the various stakeholders in the problem.

Cause of the problem
Exchanging files is an everyday need, both at home and in the office. It may happen that files are shared between different devices, for example from a phone to a laptop. To exchange files it is necessary that the devices can be connected to each other. This can be achieved through a physical link, such as external storage or a cable, or by using a third-party app.
Using a physical link is not always available or practicable. This could be, for example, if you are on the road and do not have access to an external hard drive or if you want to connect multiple devices at the same time. If this is the case, an app must be used that can send files. InsidecompanyY Slack or Gmail will be used to exchange files.
However, this does not always run smoothly, as both parties must have the relevant applications installed. This can sometimes lead to compatibility issues between devices running different operating systems. Sharing files between different Apple products can be easily done via AirDrop, but if you want to share a file from an Android phone, for example, this is not possible via AirDrop as this app is only supported on Apple operating systems. InsidecompanyY the participants have the freedom to use an operating system of their choice, which means that there are all kinds of devices available with different operating systems.
In addition, many platforms require an account, so if the person you want to share the file with will have to register if they don't already have an account for the platform. Logging in with an existing account can also require a lot of effort because you need to find the account password when a password manager is used to store unique and complex passwords.
In addition, the platforms often restrict the size of the file that is shared. Slack sets a hard limit of 1GB, with Gmail this limit is even lower, namely 25 MB. The platforms impose these restrictions as their application becomes responsible for storing and distributing the file. Because of this, large file transfers are often not available for free, or are limited to a certain file size before the user has to pay to exchange the file. This can be an obstacle, especially when there is frequent exchange of large files.
In addition, problems can arise when sharing smaller files via existing applications. To reduce the amount of data that the platforms send, many platforms use compression algorithms. Unfortunately, this comes with data loss, resulting in a noticeably lower quality of images, videos and music files. As a result, these files often have a visibly or audibly lower quality than the original.

The provided text for the "Problem Analysis" section does an effective job of identifying and outlining the issue of file sharing within companyY, including its causes, current consequences, and implications. The logical flow from identifying the problem to exploring its consequences provides a clear narrative that sets the stage for discussing potential solutions and relevant stakeholders. Below are some pieces of feedback aimed to strengthen the section and ensure alignment with the provided structure:

1. **Clarity**: The text provides a clear explanation of the issues with file sharing encountered by companyY employees—this is a good foundation for the problem analysis.

2. **Structure Compliance**: The structure is followed as outlined in the initial suggestion, with sub-sections clearly delineated for the cause of the problem, its formulation, and potential solutions. Ensure that sections 3.2 and 3.5 are further elaborated upon in the full report to complete the picture.

3. **Context**: Consider further elaboration in section 3.3 on the specific context of companyY—how vital is file sharing to daily operations? What specific workflows or processes are most impacted?

4. **Cause and Consequences**: The section correctly identifies the causes of the file-sharing issue, including the lack of a universal solution and the limitations of existing solutions (compatibility, size limits, quality loss, etc.). Make sure that the consequences are also discussed in terms of how they impact productivity, security, and efficiency. This will weight the problem's seriousness and justify the need for a solution.

5. **Possible Solutions**: While alluded to, this portion should discuss the various solutions that were considered before selecting the best course of action. Include why some were dismissed and others pursued, looking at feasibility, cost, and alignment with companyY's goals and values.

6. **Stakeholders**: You mention that stakeholders will be identified in section 3.5; ensure that there's an in-depth discussion of who these stakeholders are (e.g., employees, IT department, management, clients), how they are affected by the problem, and their needs and requirements when it comes to a solution.

7. **Evidence and Data**: If available, it would strengthen the report to include quantitative data or evidence (e.g., surveys, downtime reports, frequency of file-sharing issues) to underscore the problem's impact.

8. **Size Restriction Workarounds**: Discuss any current measures or workarounds that employees might be using to overcome the file size restrictions, and the inefficiencies or risks associated with these.

9. **Alignment with Solution**: Finally, ensure that there is a clear line of alignment from the problem analysis to the proposed solutions, showing how the solutions directly address the core issues identified.

By adopting these suggestions, you will enhance the comprehensiveness of the problem analysis, making clear the gravity of the issue faced by companyY and setting up a strong justification for the need to find a robust file-sharing solution that caters to the diverse technological environment of the company.

give me just the feedback "Issue
Exchanging files from device to device has several limitations and challenges. Including a fixed maximum on the size of the file, which means that files exceeding this limit cannot be shared. Popular platforms where people frequently exchange files, such as Slack, email and WhatsApp, compress the files that are sent, leading to quality loss that often causes images to be of poor quality compared to the original file. In addition, other platforms impose limits on file processing speed through throttling. As a result, the exchange of files is not as fast as the sender's internet speed allows. In addition, privacy can be a concern when using a third-party service, as the user must trust that the shared files are not stored in an unknown location, especially if the contents of these files contain sensitive information.

Solution directions
To improve the flow of file exchange, various solution directions can be envisioned. The exchange of files can be tackled in different ways, in order to find a suitable solution, a suitable solution must be considered. The solutions must take into account the current problems surrounding the exchange of files mentioned in paragraph 3.2.

Client-server file sharing
First of all, we can look at the use of a client-server architecture. One client sends the file to the server, after which the recipient can download the file from the server, see figure 3. However, this is not a suitable solution to consider because the same limitations will occur as those arising from the problem analysis, only the person responsible for the capacity and storage will be with a self-hosted server instead of with an external cloud provider.


Figure 3: client server architecture

Peer-to-Peer file sharing
The second solution considered was a peer-to-peer form of file sharing. With this variant, the receiver and sender are directly connected, see figure 4. Approaching this way has a number of advantages: the speed is not dependent on a server, the server is only responsible for helping to set up the peer to-peer connection between users.

Feedback on "Issue":

The "Issue" section succinctly outlines the core challenges faced when exchanging files across devices using popular platforms. You cover several key pain points, including file size restrictions, quality loss due to compression, file transfer speed limitations, and privacy concerns. This overview provides a solid understanding of what needs to be addressed.

However, to further strengthen this section:

- **Specific Examples**: Offer concrete examples of situations where these problems have affected workflows or outputs. This will bring the issue to life and help stakeholders understand its implications.
- **Quantitative Data**: If possible, include data or statistics to quantify the extent of the problem, such as the frequency of issues encountered or the average time delay due to throttling.
- **User Experience**: Discuss the user experience and how these problems affect the daily operations of individuals within the organization. Do these issues cause frustration or hinder productivity?
- **Security Concerns**: Elaborate on privacy issues, perhaps providing an example of a security breach or a potential risk scenario when sensitive information is involved.

Feedback on "Solution directions":

The "Solution directions" section begins to explore potential solutions but seems to lack a complete analysis. The discussion around client-server architecture quickly dismisses this option without offering much detail on potential ways to mitigate the limitations mentioned.

For a more thorough analysis, consider the following:

- **Alternatives to Client-Server**: It’s crucial to explain why despite its limitations, the client-server model is a common and often viable file-sharing solution. You could discuss potential ways to address its drawbacks, such as increasing server capacity or implementing security measures.
- **Peer-to-Peer Considerations**: While you mention the advantages of a peer-to-peer solution, it would be beneficial to also discuss any drawbacks or challenges, such as scalability, reliability, and security. How might these be overcome to ensure a robust file-sharing solution?
- **Figure References**: Ensure that the figures mentioned (Figures 3 and 4) are included and properly labeled in the final report. Additionally, the text should clearly describe these figures and how they visually represent the architectures discussed.
- **Hybrid Solutions**: Is there a possibility of a hybrid solution that combines elements of different architectures for an optimal outcome?
- **Additional Options**: Solutions beyond the two mentioned—such as distributed file systems, blockchain-based alternatives, or the use of file compression software that minimizes quality loss—should be considered to provide a comprehensive view of the options available.
- **Evaluation Criteria**: Define the criteria that will be used to evaluate the proposed solutions. For instance, cost-effectiveness, ease of implementation, user-friendliness, and security should be factored into the decision-making process.

By incorporating these suggestions, the report will demonstrate a thorough exploration of potential solutions to the file-sharing problems described, rationalizing the most appropriate choice for the organization.

Working methods
Development methodology
To achieve the intended goal of the project, a development methodology will be established. The chosen development methodology for this project is Agile. Agile is a project management technique in which the project is divided into phases and the emphasis is on flexibility and the ability to respond to changes. Agile's motto is based on the idea that it is not possible to fully predict all the details and requirements in advance. That is why we work with short iterations, where the team continuously plans, executes and reflects on the iteration to improve the process.
The common framework Kanban was chosen to apply Agile. Kanban offers more flexibility in planning compared to Scrum. Kanban uses a pull-based system, where new items are moved from the backlog to the “doing” column as soon as a developer has completed a task. Since the time it takes to implement a user story can vary, Kanban offers the ability to process a different number of tickets per week. This creates a more efficient workflow and makes it possible to increase productivity when necessary. Kanban's flexibility and adaptability allows the project team to adapt to changing circumstances and priorities, making the overall project scope more manageable.
In addition, to guarantee the quality of the application and detect errors at an early stage, test driven development (TDD) will be used within the application. With TDD, tests are written for a function before the function is implemented. With TDD, the expected results are specified in advance, after which the function will be implemented and continuously validated based on the written tests. This can provide certainty about a properly functioning implementation of a function.

Version control
The versions of the software are managed using git. A git branch will be used where themain branch will serve to maintain the code that is ready for the end user. Thedevelopment branch will serve as the branch from which the features will be built out, branches will be added with thefeat- prefix in which new additions will be developed. The code from the feature branches will come together again in thedevelopment branch see figure 5.

Feedback on "Working Methods":

**Development Methodology**

The description of the development methodology provides a clear rationale for adopting Agile and succinctly explains its benefits, such as flexibility and adaptability. You correctly highlight the importance of being responsive to changes, which is a central tenet of Agile methodologies.

The choice of Kanban as a framework is well-justified. Your explanation captures the essence of Kanban—its pull-based system and flexibility in managing workload variations. It's good to see you note how Kanban can enhance productivity and adapt to shifting priorities.

Including Test-Driven Development (TDD) in your methodology is also a strong addition. By emphasizing the importance of writing tests before implementing functions and the benefits this brings, you're showcasing a commitment to quality and reliable code from the early stages of the project.

However, consider these enhancements to solidify the section:

- **Agile Implementation**: Expand on how Agile will be tailored specifically for this project. Will there be daily stand-ups, retrospectives, or sprint reviews? How will you ensure continuous feedback and incorporate stakeholder involvement?
  
- **Kanban Mechanics**: You may wish to delve a little deeper into how Kanban will be implemented. Will there be WIP (Work In Progress) limits? How will you handle the prioritization of backlog items?

- **TDD Approach**: Provide a bit more detail on how TDD will be integrated into your development workflow. For example, will there be a particular unit test framework used? How will the test results feed back into the development process?

**Version Control**

You clearly establish git as the version control system of choice. The strategy to leverage different branches for various stages of development demonstrates foresight and an understanding of best practices.

The explanation of the branching strategy is coherent, with main and development branches serving distinct purposes. The addition of the feature branches with the `feat-` prefix is a useful convention that will likely aid in organization and clarity.

To further refine this section:

- **Branching Strategy**: More details on the branching strategy could be beneficial. Will there be regular merges? How will you manage conflicts between branches? Clarifying these practices will strengthen the report.

- **Figure Reference**: As with the previous section, ensure that referenced figures are included, properly labeled, and described within the text. For Figure 5, an accompanying explanation can illustrate the branching and merging workflow and the role each branch plays.

- **Code Review and Merging**: Consider including procedures for code review and merging. Will there be pull requests or merge requests? Who is responsible for reviewing and approving changes?

These detailed descriptions will give readers, especially non-technical stakeholders, a more thorough understanding of the development processes and how they foster the delivery of a high-quality product. Adding these refinements will also demonstrate an awareness of best practices in software development.

Planning & Analysefase
During the first phase of the project, problems are identified and investigated, so that shortcomings are identified at this time. In addition, the various interested parties will be examined, so that the solution can be tailored to the intended target group. In addition, research will be conducted into possible solutions to determine how the problem could possibly be resolved. Finally, within the analysis phase, the various interested parties will be contacted to determine their various interests and on this basis to formulate requirements that the application must meet. In addition, an objective and scope will be drawn up so that the development of the application can be achieved within the available time.

Feedback on "Planning & Analysis Phase":

**Comprehensiveness**

The description of the planning and analysis phase outlines a systematic approach that aligns well with the initial stages of a project's life cycle. It shows an understanding of the importance of identifying problems, undertaking research, including stakeholders, and setting objectives and scope.

**Clarity**

Your description is concise, yet it covers all the key aspects of planning and analysis. However, clarity could be enhanced with specifics on how these tasks will be accomplished. Will there be interviews, surveys, workshops, or use of analytical tools?

**Detail on Research**

Researching possible solutions is fundamental in this phase, but it would be helpful to have more information -- for example, what criteria will be used to evaluate these solutions? How will you ensure that the research covers all potential options?

**Stakeholder Engagement**

You mention that stakeholders will be identified and consulted to determine their interests. Expanding on how you'll document and prioritize these interests could be beneficial. Will there be a stakeholder matrix or other prioritization techniques used to ensure that their needs are appropriately weighted in the decision-making process?

**Objective and Scope**

While objectives and scope are crucial, a mention of how these will align with the company's broader strategic goals would be valuable. How will you ensure that the project scope is feasible, given the identified objectives and the available time?

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Structure**: Consider providing subheadings or bullet points for clarity and to delineate different activities within the phase.

- **Methods & Tools**: Include specific methodologies, tools, or techniques that will be used for problem identification and stakeholder analysis.

- **Success Criteria**: Mention how success will be measured in this phase. For example, a complete list of requirements, a fully mapped stakeholder landscape, or a selection of tested solution options.

- **Timeframe**: Define a rough timeline for the planning and analysis phase to give readers a sense of the project's pacing.

- **Record Keeping**: Explain how findings and decisions will be documented – will there be a formal requirements document or a stakeholder register?

- **Communication Plan**: Briefly outline how communication with stakeholders will take place and how often.

By incorporating these elements, the report will better convey the depth and rigor of the planning and analysis phase, setting a solid foundation for the subsequent stages of the project. It will articulate the intention to develop a well-researched, stakeholder-informed solution within a pre-defined scope and timeframe.

Design phase
During the second phase of the project, the application designs will be created. During the design phase, the application will be designed based on previous research. During the design phase, the technology that could be used for the application will also be considered. We will look at which aspects are needed for the application and the appropriate diagrams will be created so that the application can be implemented based on these diagrams. Based on the collected requirements and the stakeholders, wireframes will be created that will be discussed together withcompanyY’s UX-team.

Implementation phase
During the implementation phase, the product will be developed based on the previously made designs and the research that has occurred beforehand. During this phase, an update is made from time to time to the designs that have been made, if they have changed.

Test phase
In connection with the chosen test strategy, the test phase will be incorporated into the implementation phase, see chapter 9.1. During this phase, tests are implemented so that the quality of the application is guaranteed and errors are found and resolved at an early stage. Various tests will be performed such as integration testing and unit testing.

Deploymentfase
During the deployment phase, the application is prepared to go into production. The infrastructure will be prepared, the application will be installed on the infrastructure. The application is made available to the end user. A CI pipeline will be set up so that the deployment of the application is automated.

Aftercare phase
Due to the duration of the project, there is no aftercare phase in which functionalities can be improved by the original developer. To ensure the transfer of the application runs smoothly, the application is extensively documented so that another developer can continue working on the project if necessary.

Feedback on "Design Phase":

**Detail on Design Process**

The design phase is adequately outlined, describing the progression from research to creating application designs, and the involvement of the UX team. However, a more detailed explanation of the design methodologies — such as user-centered design, design thinking, or service design — could be beneficial.

**Inclusion of Technology Stack**

Considering the technology stack during the design phase is vital and well-positioned. It would add value to provide examples of the types of technology considered (e.g., programming languages, frameworks, databases) and the reasons behind their selection.

**Stakeholder Engagement**

The collaboration with companyY’s UX team and the creation of wireframes are good practices. Mentioning specific ways the wireframes will be validated, such as through user testing or stakeholder reviews, could enrich this section.

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Design Deliverables**: Clarify what diagrams will be created (e.g., system architecture, ER diagrams, flow diagrams) and how they contribute to the implementation phase.
- **Technology Assessment**: Discuss how the technology will be evaluated based on requirements, performance, scalability, and maintainability.
- **Feedback Loops**: Outline the process for feedback on designs and how iterations will be managed.

Feedback on "Implementation Phase":

**Dynamic Design Updates**

It's positive to see acknowledgment that designs may evolve during implementation. However, providing a structured approach to managing and documenting these changes would demonstrate proactive planning.

**Communication and Collaboration**

Mention collaborative efforts during the implementation phase, such as Agile ceremonies or code reviews, to underscore the dynamic and collaborative nature of the project.

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Change Management**: Describe the process for incorporating design updates. How will changes be assessed and approved?
- **Progress Tracking**: Explain how progress will be tracked and communicated to stakeholders.

Feedback on "Test Phase":

**Integration with Implementation**

The integration of testing with the implementation phase is in line with modern software development practices. Delineate who is responsible for writing and executing tests and when tests will be run.

**Testing Strategies**

Expanding on the testing strategies to be employed would give stakeholders a clear understanding of how quality is assured.

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Testing Tools and Frameworks**: Provide information on the tools and frameworks that will be used for different types of testing.
- **Continuous Testing**: Discuss continuous testing and how it will be integrated into the CI/CD pipeline.

Feedback on "Deployment Phase":

**Infrastructure Preparation**

The description of infrastructure preparation and application installation is clear. Elaboration on considerations such as scalability, monitoring, and security would be beneficial.

**Automation**

The mention of setting up a CI pipeline for automated deployment is an excellent addition. Including the goals of automation — such as faster release cycles or reduced human error — could add context.

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Rollout Strategy**: Discuss the strategy for rolling out the application to users, such as a phased rollout or a feature flagging approach.
- **Monitoring and Scaling**: Detail how the application will be monitored post-deployment and the strategy for scaling if necessary.

Feedback on "Aftercare Phase":

**Documentation**

Emphasizing the importance of documentation for ensuring maintainability is important and well-placed. Detail what types of documentation will be created (e.g., code comments, API docs, user manuals).

**Knowledge Transfer**

The knowledge transfer plan is critical for sustainability. Articulating how these handover sessions will be structured can validate the thoroughness of the transition.

**Actionable Suggestions**

- **Documentation Standards**: Define the standards or templates that will be used for documentation to ensure consistency.
- **Continuity Plan**: Outline a continuity plan that addresses potential risks or knowledge gaps after the project handover.

Overall, each phase is logically described, with a clear process that goes from design to deployment and aftercare. Incorporating these suggestions will enhance the clarity, depth, and comprehensiveness of the project planning and execution strategy.

Physical view



Figure 18: Deployment diagram

Figure 18 shows the deployment diagram. The diagram shows a number of parts of the application, the user with his device, the DNS service (Route 53), Cloudfront (CDN) to which the DNS refers, which will then connect to one of the Cloudfront edges that are in near the user, the Lightsail container service on which a containerized version of the backend runs and finally the ECR6 see where the docker images are pushed to and pulled from. The deployment diagram shows the protocols used for communication between the different instances. The protocols used are HTTPS for the frontend and WSS for the backend, both protocols use TLS to ensure the security of the application (NF5, NF6, NF7).
6Elastic Container Registry

Feedback on "Physical View":

The "Physical View" section references a deployment diagram (Figure 18), which indicates that you've provided a visual representation of the application infrastructure. This is an excellent practice as it offers an at-a-glance understanding of how components are distributed and interact. 

**Positive Aspects**:
- **Clear Component Breakdown**: You have outlined key components, such as the user's device, DNS service, CDN, and container services, which is integral to understanding the deployment infrastructure.
- **Protocols Mentioned**: Identifying the communication protocols (HTTPS and WSS) and the use of TLS for security is a good level of detail that underscores a focus on secure communication.

**Areas for Improvement**:

WebRTC security
The WebRTC protocol has authenticity, integrity and confidentiality (NF5, NF6, NF7). Within the WebRTC this is achieved through the use of the DTLS7 protocol and SRTP8 protocol. DTLS helps negotiate a session, the protocol is used to initiate the first handshake of WebRTC
7Datagram Transport Layer Security
8Secure Real-Time Transfer Protocol



to complete so that information such as ICE candidates can be exchanged safely. SRTP is then used for media exchange. SRTP is used for sending media because, unlike DTLS, SRTP has symmetric encryption, which makes data processing faster.

WebRTC Datachannels
The WebRTC's data channels use the Stream Control Transmission Protocol (SCTP) RFC 4960, a protocol intended as an alternative to UDP and TCP. Data Channels are an abstraction of this protocol. The data channels have a message size limit of 64 KiB. To send files larger than 64 KiB, the files can be sent in chunks. The file will be sent in chunks at the receiving end and saved in chunks to the disk, if the browser supports the FileSystem Writable FileStream API. The advantage of saving in chunks to disk is the fact that you are not dependent on the amount of memory on the device.

WebRTC Topologies

The WebRTC protocol establishes a direct connection between two users. There are all kinds of strategies to scale up the connections to large numbers of users. The developer must take into account how he sets up connections if this has to be scaled to hundreds of users. The implementation strategies can be divided into two architecture categories: peer-to-peer and client-server.



Figuur 20: One-to One connectivity WebRTC

The one-to-one is the basic situation that occurs on which the other connectivity typologies build on, see figure 20. The agents are connected to each other and have the option for bi-directional communication. This connection will be used when one agent wants to exchange a file with another agent.



Figuur 21: Full mesh connectivity WebRTC


With full mesh9 connectivity, peer-to-peer connections are set up between all other agents, this means that every agent will be connected to every other agent, see figure 21. Being directly connected per agent offers the advantage that a specific encoding can be chosen for each agent depending on the ice candidates that the agent offers. However, this also has the disadvantage that it may happen that the same encoding is not reused, and error handling is also more complex because you have to keep track of whether you have lost a connection with a peer or with all peers.

Figure 22: WebRTC hybrid mesh

The hybrid mesh strategy, see figure 22, is an alternative that solves some of the problems of the full mesh. It alleviates the amount of bandwidth required from the sender because the other agents will act as a relay and forward the files to the other agents connected to them. Disadvantages of this approach are mainly the lack of control/certainty of who receives the sent files. In addition, there will be increased latency with each hop between agents.

Figure 23: WebRTC SFU

Selective Forwarding Unit (SFU) is a strategy in which the problems of full mesh are solved in a different way, namely a client/server model, see figure 23. Each WebRTC peer connects to the SFU after which they send their data to it. the SFU is responsible for redistribution to the other agent. The challenge of building an SFU is in the Congestion Control, Error correction and performance.


Figuur 24: WebRTC Multi-point conferencing unit
9A mesh network is a local area network topology in which the infrastructure nodes (i.e. bridges, switches, and other infrastructure devices) connect directly



A multi-point conferencing unit is a client/server architecture just like SFU. However, there is a difference in how the connections are sent to the users, the different signaling is multiplexed, all streams are combined into one output stream, see figure 24.
Full mesh connectivity was chosen, as this topology prevents message spoofing as with hybrid mesh you have to rely on other users in the network. To avoid the need for a server, it was decided not to use the client/server strategy.

To test
This chapter explains how the application tests are determined and carried out. The test strategy is explained in section 9.1. The test plan is explained in paragraph 9.2. In paragraph

Test strategy
Tests are written to ensure the quality of the application. The test strategy aims to minimize risks and guarantee reliable operation of the application.
Given the duration of the project, it was decided to focus the tests on the functional requirements, see section 6.1. The non-functional requirements will have a lower priority given the time and duration of the project.

Unit test
Within the project, the functional requirements will be tested through unit testing. Within the hexagonal architecture it is important to test the core of the application. The core of the application contains the business logic and domain logic of the application, which are independent of external factors such as the drive. The business logic of the application can be tested by isolating the logic by mocking the external dependencies, this disconnects the logic from the surrounding infrastructure.
Since the unit tests are completely isolated from the surrounding dependencies, it is possible to perform these tests in the CI/CD. Therefore, to reduce potential risks associated with changes and extensions to the code, the tests are performed within the CI/CD process. By carrying out these tests during the CI/CD process, it can be determined at an early stage whether the functionalities have changed undesirably.

Test integration
In contrast to the use of unit tests, when writing integration tests, the integration between the different layers of the system is tested. With the hexagonal architecture, this is the interaction of the core of the application with the ports and adapters. Since it is important to test whether the flow of the application works, integration tests are written. Within integration testing, the joint operation and flow of the application is tested, whether the interaction between the different components runs smoothly.

The End-to-End test
Within the project it was decided not to implement end-to-end testing, as setting up such tests is time-consuming and offers limited added value. User scenarios are simulated in the backend of the application through integration testing. When validating frontend functionalities, it is often necessary to involve a second user to fully verify the requirements. Given the project duration and the frequent iterations in the implementation of the application's functions, it is considered redundant to use both end-to-end testing and integration testing. Modifying the code requires changes in both end-to-end testing and integration testing, which does not improve the efficiency of the testing process.

This tool, developed by Google, offers various audits on a website. “It has audits for performance accessibility, progressive web apps, SEO and more” Google, g.d. In addition to assessing accessibility, the tool also provides other valuable insights for the application, such as meeting the requirements to be installed as a progressive web application and the overall performance of the application's front end.

Development Street
During deployment, a development street is used. This development street, also known as CI/CD Pipeline, will go through various phases before the frontend and backend of the application. The different phases that will be completed will be explained below.

Test phase
The first phase to be completed within the pipeline will be the test phase, within this phase the unit tests specified in chapter 9 will be performed. This phase is run before the solution is deployed so that the code is not hosted if a functionality no longer works as expected.
The tests will be carried out when a pull request is opened, so that the developer can see that no functionalities have stopped working before he merges the code into the branch or whether the code still functions as intended.

Code quality phase
Qodana is a static analysis tool developed by Jetbrains. With new merges, the code is analyzed and possible errors in the code are detected. Since Qodana was developed by Jetbrains, it integrates with the IDEs also developed by Jetbrians. This makes it easy to navigate directly to the correct file line in the IDE based on the detected errors. A quality gate, a quality requirement before the code is ready to deploy, can be set up in the pipeline. Within Qodana, the quality gate can be a limit to the amount of errors within the file.

Build fase
In the build phase of the development street, the code is compiled. If the code succeeds in building the code, it is stored in an artifact. An artifact is the collection of files created in the build process. These can then be reused in another phase. In addition, the artifacts can be viewed and downloaded so that they can be debugged if necessary.

Deployment fase
If the phases that pass the deployment phase pass, the deploy phase will be executed. In the deployment phase the artifacts will be sent to the hosting. The deployment phase will be performed only on the main branch, so that only code that is ready to go into production is put online.

Setting up services on AWS is time-consuming, the developer must manually create the services using the provided graphical user interface (GUI) of the service, for example: AWS, Azure or Google Cloud. Terminology clarification
This report provides an in-depth analysis of the sync tool development process, often referring to terms such as “Teams Notifications,” the “Teams List,” “DevOps Notification Board,” and “DevOps Notifications.” To avoid possible confusion, it is important to clarify the specific, technical meaning of these terms:
Teams notifications: Indicates individual items in a SharePoint list that serve as notifications within Teams. These items are managed in a specific SharePoint list known as the "Teams list".
Teams list: Refers to the SharePoint list where the Teams notifications are recorded. It serves as the central hub for managing and updating all notifications.
DevOps-meldingenbord: Corresponding to Azure Boards, DevOps notification boards represent the place where DevOps Work Items are tracked and managed. These Work Items include several variations that are important for the development process.
The DevOps message: Indicates specific Work Items within the DevOps notification board, which range from tasks to user stories and bugs.

Synchronization flow
Teams – OPAL – DevOps relationship
To successfully realize a product, it is essential to properly map the relationships between the entities involved. We have already established in previous chapters that OPAL is the internal project management system and a Teams and DevOps project can be created for projects. Previously, it was the rule that a Teams was created for each project and that they also had their own DevOps, but nowadays it is also possible that multiple OPAL projects are linked to one team. This is possible, for example, because a customer has a project in development at companyX and has purchased a permanent product such as the Diamond Online Forms.
	For our sync tool, this means that we must take into account that not all notifications from a Teams list must be sent to one DevOps project. To determine which DevOps the notifications go to, I have added an extra column in the projects table called “ApplicationName”. In the Teams list there is already a field “Application” from which you can choose from a set of standard - values, these values ​​are filled with the project name by default when generating the Teams in OPAL. The idea behind the new “ApplicationName” column is that when an incoming teams notification comes from a list linked to multiple OPAL projects, the project is searched for on the “Application” and the notification is sent to the associated DevOps.
Desired operation of sync tool
[Desired operation of sync tool]

Teams - DevOps Mapping
As described above in the operation of the sync tool, an incoming Teams notification must be added or updated in the DevOps project linked to the combination of the associated OPAL project and the chosen ApplicationName in the Teams notification. Because the objects of the two platforms are not identical and not all fields apply in the two systems, they must be converted into a suitable form. In a normal situation this can easily be done by casting the objects to each other and filling in the fields, but in the case of Teams & DevOps this works slightly differently.
Problem
A Teams notification always has the same fields, but a DevOps notification does not. This is because Azure offers the ability to assign a project to a process. The processes are used to manage DevOps notifications and are essential for optimizing workflow and project success. Azure offers out-of-the-box standard processes Basic, Agile, Scrum & CMMI. In these processes, the notification types are defined with the corresponding fields for each type (Microsoft Learn, 2023). This system ensures that the notification system can be adjusted to the needs of a project, but creates a critical problem for the synchronization tool. Because the fields and possible values ​​of a DevOps notification can differ across notification types, but also across processes, it is not possible to cast a Teams notification to a fixedly defined form.
Solution
The difference between the two platforms lies at the level of the notifications, their fields and values. To find out which value belongs in a field of a DevOps notification with a specific WorkItemType in a specific process and visa-versa, we can persist this information in the database to retrieve the information later when used. The database tables consist of four levels regarding the mapping between the two systems: DevOps Process -> DevOps WorkItemType -> FieldMappings -> ValueMappings [7.1 Database Structure]. Now that we have a way to capture the mappings, we still need to implement a functionality that makes it possible to convert a Teams notification to a DevOps notification and vice versa.

Call Converting
Flow
Converting a report from one platform to another requires a number of steps, some of which are initial and some repetitive. We start with the input. This can be a Teams notification or DevOps notification, for the explanation we will use a Teams notification as an example. For the conversion, we start with a few entities: The Teams notification, a DevOps process of the project where the DevOps notification ends up and the desired DevOps notification type. With this information, we look up the records in the database that match the data of the DevOps process and notification type. This will allow us to look up the mappings later. An activity diagram can be viewed for support and visualization in [Figure 1 - Activity Diagram Call-Converter]

Not all fields of the notification need to be synchronized to DevOps because this is platform-specific or not desired by the user (e.g.: List-related information). It is important that we determine in advance which fields will be mapped. Because Teams notifications have fixed fields, we can define this in the code.

Here we see that we want to convert the fields: title, description, priority and status.
We use the basic information to convert the notification and this works as follows: For each field we look up the field mapping in the persistent DevOps process and notification type so that we know what the field is called in DevOps (e.g.: “Status” -> “State”) and we look up the value mappings with which we can look up the field value. As shown below, there may be no field mapping for a field and a specific notification type because it is not present and we ignore the Teams field in the conversion.


 

Now that we have found the name of the DevOps field, we still have to determine which value will end up in the field. One or more value mappings are linked to the field mapping. These can be two scenarios:
There is one record whose Teams and DevOps value is '*', this means that the value of the Teams field must be transferred to DevOps
There are one or more records present, here we look for the mapping with our Teams value and put the corresponding DevOps value into our DevOpsMapping object.



By doing these steps for all Teams fields, we end up with an object that contains a list of combinations of DevOps field names and values ​​specific to the incoming DevOps process and notification type, our DevOps notification.

To go from a DevOps notification to a Teams notification, the implementation is slightly different. Because we do not know the field names of the notification, we cannot determine the convertible properties, as with a Teams notification, and cannot assume a defined object as input. To remain flexible, in addition to a DevOps process and notification type, we receive as input a list of combinations of field names and values ​​that must be mapped to a Teams notification. Here too we will try to find the corresponding field mapping and value mappings for each field.


Default-mapping
In the absence of a value mapping for a notification field you could crash the program, because there is a field mapping present we know that we need the field in the desired notification and we cannot fill it with information. Another option is to implement the functionality of a default value, and use this value with an empty input field or absence of associated value mapping. This aspect contributes to the flexibility of the sync tool and reduces the chance of notification conversion failure.

harePoint Webhook
Operation
The expected operation of the sync tool is mainly that the actions are automated and this does not have to be done by a companyX employee. Having an implementation for adding or updating a notification in the other system is very nice, but to initiate this process a trigger must still be present. A solution could be to periodically synchronize the notifications regardless of whether adjustments have been made, but this has the disadvantage that it may take a while before the information is synchronized. To solve this you could reduce the period between synchronizations, but that will use unnecessary resources and reduce the performance of the OPAL system.

A smart solution is to use webhooks. A webhook is, at its core, a user-defined Http callback that enables real-time communication between different web applications. They allow applications to subscribe to certain events and receive notifications when these events occur. The automated, asynchronous updates between applications eliminate the need for constant manual intervention or repetitive polling. (AppMaster, 2023)
By using webhooks that listen to new teams notifications that are added or existing ones that are changed and send our API a notification when a modification has taken place, we can initiate synchronization in a resource-efficient, performance-saving on-demand manner. SharePoint offers webhooks for various scenarios and resources, the most popular being for document libraries and lists. Customers' Teams notifications are maintained in SharePoint lists (H2.3 Terminology clarification), making this a suitable solution for our tool.
Manage subscription
Before we can receive webhook notifications, we first need to create a webhook. Here are some requirements from SharePoint and our implementation. SharePoint expects a valid resource on which the subscription should be created, a URL where the notifications should be sent and an expiration date. When requesting to create a subscription, SharePoint sends a validation request to the URL to ensure valid operation. From our tool, the requirements are synchronization-oriented. A webhook subscription should only be created when there is a Teams & DevOps associated with the specified OPAL project, because getting notifications of notifications that we cannot sync anywhere is not useful and a waste of resources. To validate the origin of the notifications, a so-called ClientState is also added. This ClientState is a random string that will be sent with every notification. From our implementation we can then check whether the ClientState matches and ignore or process the notification.
	In addition to creating a SharePoint webhook subscription, the API also supports the option to retrieve or delete a subscription. These actions can be invoked from the front-end application through the use of a simple switch allowing the user to easily manage subscriptions for the OPAL projects.

Extension of subscription
Unfortunately, the expiration date of a SharePoint webhooks subscription can be set to a maximum of 180 days. This is a choice Microsoft made, probably to remove inactive subscriptions that are forgotten by the user and save resources. We do not want an employee to have to manually create it again every 180 days from the moment a subscription was created, but for the subscription to always exist. One way to achieve this is by periodically renewing the notification. When we send an HttpRequest to create a subscription on a resource where it already exists, it is extended with the new expiration date.
	A JobService project is also present in the OPAL back-end. This job service will run the deployments periodically depending on the settings stored for individual job services in the database. In our API controller for the TeamsWebhook there is an endpoint that returns for an OPAL project whether it currently has a SharePoint webhook subscription. A written job service will check every four months for all existing projects whether there is a subscription and extend it again by 180 days. This ensures the user of constant operation of the sync tool, consistency of data between the two platforms and gives the product owner at least two months to resolve an issue if it occurs before a subscription can expire.
Processing changes
Unfortunately, when sending a notification, SharePoint does not provide the details of the modified notifications, but a list of resources to which adjustments have been made. This can allow SharePoint to bundle notifications for changed resources and notifications into one HttpRequest instead of sending multiple times with information from a single resource or notification. We will therefore first have to retrieve the adjusted notifications before we can do anything with them. SharePoint has its own system for this that works as follows: When adjusting a teams notification, adding, modifying or removing, SharePoint stores a data point in a list of changes that includes the ID of the changed item, the save the moment of change and a unique ChangeToken. From an HttpClient such as Postman or the CSOM the user can retrieve a list of changes and use the ID of the notification to retrieve them second from the teams list. A reference implementation is visualized in [Figure 2 - SharePoint Webhook Reference Implementation Flow]
	For the sync tool we only want to process new changes and not the ones we have already processed. This is where the ChangeToken comes in handy. SharePoint offers the option of retrieving changes that occurred after a certain ChangeToken, in addition to retrieving all changes. An extra column “LatestTeamsChangeToken” has been added in the projects table that keeps track of this token.
In the absence of this token (in the case of a new Teams list/SharePoint webhook subscription), the penultimate change will be retrieved and processed.



Unfortunately, the retrieved changes do not yet contain the information we need, the only useful information is the ID of the teams notification that has been modified, using the CSOM we can query the corresponding Teams list in which we are searching to teams notifications with an ID present in the list of changes.




Now that we have the Teams notifications associated with a change, we can start processing the notification. From Teams to DevOps there are three actions that can be initiated depending on the change type:
Addition
When adding a Teams notification, there will be no DevOps notification for it and we will have to create one. For the associated OPAL project, we first retrieve the process for the DevOps project and choose the -notifications type based on the Teams notification “Type”, we need this information for the notifications conversion.




After successfully adding a DevOps notification, we need to add the ID to the Teams notification so that the notifications have a reference to each other. In the notifications list there is a column called “DevOpsId” where we can enter this Id. The process of processing a new Teams call is visualized in [Figure 3 - Activity Diagram Processing Addition Teams call].

Update
Processing an updated Teams notification is quite simple, first of all we check for the presence of the “DevOpsId” field and check whether this notification is also present in the DevOps project. If this is not the case, we will empty this field in the Teams notification and cancel the synchronization.


Similar to processing a new Teams notification, we need to look up the DevOps process and notification type to convert the notification. When updating, we must take into account that the teams notification has been given a different type than the DevOps notification was initially created with, even though this will not happen often. This results in a DevOps notification from conversion that does not match the current fields in the associated DevOps notification. To resolve this, the DevOps notification type must first be adjusted before updating field values. Processing an updated Teams call is visualized in [Figure 6 - Activity Diagram Processing Update Teams call].

Deletion:
When a Teams notification is deleted, the only action that needs to be taken is to remove the reference to the Teams notification in the associated DevOps notification. This prevents a Teams notification from being found when synchronizing the DevOps notification and ensures data consistency. One problem we are encountering here is that based on the change we cannot retrieve the Teams notification from the Teams list because it has been deleted. As a result, we also do not know the number of the associated DevOps notification. One way to look up the DevOps notification is to look for a combination of the ID of the Teams notification and the unique URL of the notification in the DevOps projects linked to the Teams list.

DevOps Webhook
Operation
Like SharePoint [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation], DevOps offers support for webhooks that aim for automated and real-time communication between different systems. This solution prevents the implementation of a periodic synchronization by listening for specific events in the DevOps platform and notifying the registered systems.
A webhook is essentially a user-defined HTTP callback that subscribes to specific events. Whenever a change occurs to a DevOps work item, a notification is automatically sent to our API. This process is activated without the need for manual intervention, enabling efficient and up-to-date synchronization between Teams, OPAL and DevOps.

The use of DevOps webhooks in the sync tool brings the same benefits as the SharePoint webhooks, disabled [8.6.1 (SharePoint Webhook) Operation] and contributes to the quality and reliability of the tool. Using the DevOps webhooks is a very suitable solution for receiving and processing changed DevOps notifications.
Manage subscription
Because both SharePoint and Azure DevOps are from the Microsoft company, managing a DevOps webhook works very similar to a SharePoint webhook, but Azure offers a more diverse range of webhooks. While SharePoint can only listen to the creation and modification of Teams notifications, with DevOps it is possible to listen to development-related actions (e.g.: code pushed, pull request created, build completed, release created), but also to a notification list.
To create a webhook, DevOps expects a resource on which the subscription must be created, in our case a notification list. It also expects the specification of the entity and modification type that the subscription should listen to and a URL where the notification should be sent. Our sync tool currently only supports the ability to sync updating and deleting DevOps notifications and we choose this as the type. Unlike SharePoint webhooks, DevOps webhooks have no expiration date and the presence of a job service for renewing existing subscriptions is not applicable. Our tool requires that the project for which the webhook subscription is created has a linked Teams project, because receiving notifications that cannot be synchronized anywhere is useless.
In addition to creating a DevOps webhook subscription, the API also supports retrieving the presence or removing subscriptions. These actions can be invoked from the front-end application through the use of a switch allowing the user to easily manage the DevOps subscriptions for OPAL projects.

Webhook-loop prevention
Problem
Webhooks are called when a change occurs to a resource that has a subscription added. In our case, this is when a notification is created, modified or deleted. The webhook sends a notification to the API and it will synchronize the notification to the other platform. Imagine a scenario where we have a Teams and DevOps with synchronization to and from the platforms. This means that when changing a Teams notification, the Teams webhook triggers and the API sends a notification. The sync tool will process the change and adjust the associated DevOps notification. The DevOps webhook then triggers and sends a notification to the API, after which our sync tool adjusts the corresponding Teams notification and triggers the Teams webhook and we end up in a loop. This loop will ensure that the same notifications are constantly updated without new information until it is interrupted by human actions or an overrun of required resources.

Solution
To prevent us from processing notifications of changed notifications by the sync tool, we must keep track of which notifications have been modified by the tool. When a notification arrives, as a result, we check whether the notification has been changed by the tool and ignore the notification. As an implementation, I added two tables: WebhookModifiedTeamsCalls & WebhookModifiedDevOpsCalls [7.1 Database Structure]. After processing a notification, a unique combination of the originating notification list & the notification number is stored here. When a notification is received, the table is searched for the presence of the notification information and, if found, it is removed from the table and the sync tool will ignore the notification. The reason why it needs to be removed is because the next time the notification is possibly updated by a user we want to synchronize this modification.
Advanced Functionalities
DevOps Sync Target Location
One of the advanced features that has been added to the synchronization tool is the option to specify per OPAL project where the notifications should end up in DevOps. For example, in the “Edit Teams & DevOps” screen, a user can choose from a list of DevOps Area & Iteration locations that are persisted in the database. These locations are retrieved externally from DevOps based on the chosen OPAL project (Swagger response example shown in [Figure 7 - Swagger Example GetAreaPaths]). This way we ensure that the data chosen by the user is valid.
